news_bc.title: The Drama: Cultural Commentary or Tasteless?
news_bc.author: Taylor Finnell
news_bc.summary:
★★★☆☆

The Drama starring Robert Pattinson and Zendaya has been one of the most anticipated movies of 2026, and it was released Friday, April 3rd. Produced by A24 and directed by Kristoffer Borgli, The Drama follows a young couple living in Boston in the lead-up to their wedding, but a catastrophic secret threatens to ruin everything. 

It starts with an almost overly perfect meet-cute in a Boston Tatte Bakery and Café. Emma (Zendaya) is sitting at the counter reading a book, and when Charlie (Robert Pattinson) enters, he immediately notices her. 

As she steps away to grab her coffee, he peeks at the title of her book and quickly looks it up. He approaches her, attempting to introduce himself, but as he rambles on about the book, she completely ignores him. It turns out that Emma is deaf in one ear and was listening to music in the other, completely oblivious to his attempts at flirtation. 

As she finally notices him, realizing he had been talking, she coyly says that they can start over, and so he does, beginning their seemingly picture-perfect relationship.

From here, the movie switches between the beginning of their relationship, like breaking into the Cambridge art museum, nice dinners, drinks with friends, to the present, leading up to their wedding. 

Pattinson and Zendaya are both incredible actors and portray the attractive yet slightly awkward couple perfectly. They live in an amazing Boston apartment with fabulous jobs—she as a publishing editor and he as an art museum curator. 

Charlie is a naturally nervous man, described as constantly hyperfixating on things and wanting them to go off without a hitch. Emma seems to be more carefree and goes with the flow. On the surface, they appear to complement each other perfectly. 

One night a week before their wedding, they are finishing up dishes and drinks with two of their best friends. After many glasses of wine, the friends dare one another to share the worst thing they have ever done. 

This is where the true drama begins. Everyone’s secrets are bad, but not that bad, like locking a kid in a closet and cyberbullying a fellow student while in school. 

But Emma’s secret is not something that can be chalked up to youthful ignorance or laughed off. It is psychopathic and violent, and it forces Charlie to reanalyze everything he has ever learned about her. 

As the movie progresses, Charlie spirals into the dark secret Emma has revealed, wanting to learn every detail of what almost happened, what she almost did. Yet she is increasingly hesitant to share, which leaves Charlie to obsess over and interrogate it all alone.

The genre of this film is very hard to pin down. It reads as part dark romantic comedy, part drama, marrying comedy with hard-to-swallow questions that stay with you long after leaving.

The Drama has faced significant backlash as well as praise. Many feel that the expansive marketing and press campaigns presented it as a rom-com with some dark elements. The invitations to the premiere were sent out as wedding invitations, and the party had a massive wedding cake. 

But Emma’s secret, and inadvertently the main plot twist of the film, has drawn the most criticism from major activist groups, like March For Our Lives, which, in a recent post, wrote that “[t]he way this film has been marketed is deeply misaligned with the reality it engages.” 

The film’s admirers lean into the argument that art should explore unsettling, hard-to-answer questions even at the risk of upsetting people. 

These questions will not go away, and it is up to us how we respond to them. While the film was definitely not boring, it forces you to think and examine just how normalized certain things have become in our society. The Drama provokes and interrogates how much you can truly know someone, and the reality of tying yourself to another person for life. 

The drama is a visually appealing, entertaining film that shocks and unsettles while also making you laugh. It treads the line of absurdity, but it is hard to peel your eyes away from the screen. This is a movie that requires a post-watch debrief and maybe a glance at the content warnings before watching. 

The post The Drama: Cultural Commentary or Tasteless? appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: When Satire Hits a Nerve: The Druski Debate
news_bc.author: Nikita Leal
news_bc.summary:
For anyone who’s been on their doomscroll grind lately, you might’ve seen the controversy of Druski’s latest parody skit. Now, whether or not you’re a fan of Druski’s or not, you’ve likely seen some of his viral sketch comedy clips. From his Coulda Been House and Coulda Been Love series’ pulling in over 10 million views to his collabs with streamers like Kai Cenat and celebrities like Kevin Hart, Druski has carved out a lane for himself in comedy.

On March 25th, Druski posted an Instagram Reel that was a bit different from his usual content. It was a parody skit titled “How Conservative Women in America Act,” featuring Druski, a Black man, impressively disguised as a white woman. 

Despite the debate the video stirred up, it still blew up with 7.8 million likes, with comments praising his unhinged creativity while others bashed his alleged mockery of Erika Kirk, the widow of the recently passed Charlie Kirk. Kirk is a Republican woman who now holds the title of CEO of her late husband’s conservative organization, Turning Point USA. 

Druski’s video, while never explicitly naming her, gives the impression that he may have been inspired by Erika Kirk, especially since the costumes and makeup closely resemble her appearance. The skit mirrors some of her past appearances and speeches in a way that’s clearly meant to poke fun at her persona.

Naturally, conservatives are offended at Druski’s lack of sympathy and respect for Erika. Many people, mainly Republicans, online have even labeled Druski’s “whiteface” as racist, arguing that it’s no different from blackface. 

I don’t personally find Druski to be the funniest comedian out there, and while I’m sure he has his moments, I found this particular skit to be amusing and accurate. The backlash Druski is facing feels uncalled for––it comes off more like projection from offended conservatives rather than a real case of discrimination. 

Comparing whiteface to blackface simply doesn’t hold up because the two will never carry the same weight. Blackface has a specifically harmful history. It originated as theatrical makeup used mostly by white performers to mock and dehumanize Black people through exaggerated, insulting caricatures. That legacy is tied to prejudice, segregation, and centuries of systemic hate that portrayed white supremacy in entertainment.

A “whiteface” gag will never be as harmful as blackface because it’s rooted in exaggerating how certain forms of social privilege show up daily. In Druski’s skit, the contrast is hauntingly realistic in the scene where he orders his dog a pup cup from Starbucks immediately after standing behind a podium, fake‑crying about war casualties. 

Druski is showing Kirk’s performative activism through the disconnect between meaningful moral concern and the reality of one’s actions. The people who are the most upset are only offended because they see themselves in his mockery and recognize how ridiculous their behavior comes off. 

Even American conservative political commentator Candace Owens posted a podcast clip on TikTok reacting to Druski’s skit, clearly amused. Her reaction was especially surprising given her past work with Turning Point USA and Kirk. In the video, she calls the skit hilarious and argues that it captures how Kirk can come across as inauthentic with the way she leans on Christianity in ways that feel more like a shield than a belief. 

Comparing it to the kind of behavior where someone uses a Bible verse to justify being catty or to make themselves seem morally superior when they’re not—a pattern I’d accuse Kirk of constantly committing. 

The bit of irony here is how the upheaval of Druski’s harmless parody skit is receiving criticism that a movie like White Chicks doesn’t face. White Chicks is a comedy film that came out in 2004, admittedly before “cancel culture,” but it featured two Black brothers, Marlon and Shawn Mayans, dressed head to toe in a white woman’s outfit—skin tone and all. 

This parody is a widely beloved movie for its inane jokes and quotable lines, yet Druski’s same intent is bashed. Honestly, it seems conservatives are mad about how they’re being represented, and more especially because he’s right. 

A “whiteface” bit like this is meant to expose how social privilege shows up. It’s designed to make people uncomfortable enough to reflect on how we let prejudiced behaviors fly under the radar. 

Meanwhile, some public figures, like President Donald Trump, are suggesting that Kirk should sue Druski, which seems disproportionate to something that was meant to poke fun. 

If we’re going to talk about the right and wrongs of parodies, Scary Movie 6—another film built entirely on exaggerated characters and cultural satire—is hitting theaters soon. Are we prepared to critique all parodies equally, or only those that touch the nerves of privileged conservatives?

The post When Satire Hits a Nerve: The Druski Debate appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Newton City Council Chairs Talk Zoom Security, AI Transcripts in Public Meetings
news_bc.author: Katherine Kline
news_bc.summary:
The chairs of the Newton City Council met Monday to discuss if Zoom is a secure enough platform for council meetings and whether AI transcripts or summary notes are the best way to convey information to Newton residents. 

“The meeting format is the way to go,” said Ward 8 Councilor-at-Large David Kalis. “It is very important for the community to know who is there. It is a lot more of a community effort, and people feel more welcome than when you’re in the webinar format. I’m willing to live with the Zoom bombing, as unpleasant as they are. It’s a real public square-type format.” 

The main issue with Zoom has been the rise of “Zoom bombings,” which are when members of the public intentionally disrupt the meeting, causing it to end prematurely. For Ward 7 Councilor Lisle Baker, filtering out these interruptions and protecting the sanctity of council meetings are his top priorities.

“I am not a fan of using Zoom,” Baker said. “I’m prepared to move my committee to the extent that I can, to the webinar format, because I think the public interest is in having people hear and see what is said. That is really much more important than knowing who else is on the call, so I’m not a fan of Zoom.”

Newton’s city clerk, Drew Willison, suggested the clerk’s office monitor the number of people coming to meetings over the next month. This data can then be used to help guide the council going forward. 

“I’m wondering if the clerk’s office could keep track over the next month of how many members of the public come into our meetings,” Willison said. “We could pick the meetings that we want more people to be part of, and those meetings can be Zooms. But if we could get some data from the clerk’s office about how many people are actually coming in, that might be useful.”

The lack of clerk’s office staff was also discussed, as well as new staff who are still learning the office’s protocols for writing meeting reports. The current standard practice of the council is to post recorded meetings alongside an audio recording, explained Willison.

Ward 7 Councilor-at-Large Becky Walker Grossman expressed concerns about changing the current system and including a written transcript.

“I don’t love the idea of having to capture the mood, tone, and context of an individual counselor’s comment,” Grossman said. “And I would just also add on top of it, particularly in this time of transition with so many new members of our staff coming in, there is definitely a learning curve and a fair amount of variability.”

Ward 5 Councilor-at-Large Brittany Hume Charm suggested a compromise between the new and old systems.

“One compromise could be to have the meeting notes offer timestamps in the videos of when certain docket items were addressed,” Charm said. “So that they’re easy to find in the video.”

Ward 3 Councilor-at-Large Andrea Kelley highlighted that Newton residents are the ones requesting more information from the council, not just city officials. 

“It’s not just councilors who are looking for this information, as many of us have been hearing at office hours and talking to constituents, it’s residents who would like to see who said what,” Kelley said. “There’s a lot to be said for having a written record of a committee report, which is basically a summary of notes.”

Ward 1 Councilor-at-Large Alison Leary offered a solution based on Newton Mayor Marc Laredo’s approach to transparency.

“It would be really great to make things easier to find on the city’s web page,” Leary said. “I’m just thinking of our prior mayor’s newsletter and our current mayor’s newsletter. Those are very well received.”

The post Newton City Council Chairs Talk Zoom Security, AI Transcripts in Public Meetings appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Newton City Councilors Discuss New Emissions Regulations Under BERDO
news_bc.author: Thomas Kenneally
news_bc.summary:
Newton’s Committee of the Whole met on Monday to discuss draft regulations aimed at emissions reduction, with the Climate and Sustainability team outlining their plans for regulatory adjustments and members of the council voicing cost concerns.

The Building Emissions Reduction and Disclosure Ordinance (BERDO) requires larger buildings to report energy use with the goal of reducing greenhouse gas emissions. The program originates from the City of Boston, which implemented its first version of BERDO in 2013. 

“We must transition city buildings from fossil fuels to being fully electric,” reads the City of Newton’s website.

To achieve this goal, the Climate and Sustainability team’s BERDO administrator, Darius Bailey, outlined specific regulatory proposals along with long-term plans. 

The most concrete proposal was the Equitable Emissions Investment Fund. The fund would be supported by fines related to BERDO regulations and would be used around the city to make up for violations in emission creation, explained Bailey.

“The goal with the fund is to take that money and then invest it into other decarbonization projects,” Bailey said. 

These projects could range from work with non-profits, businesses, or even city projects. The proposal received no criticism or comment from the committee.

Another core part of BERDO’s future are Hardship Compliance Plans (HCP), which give building owners an opportunity for regulatory relief if granted. It is not a simple exemption from BERDO but a temporary alleviation from requirements for a set amount of time. 

Bailey explained that the scope of reasons for exemption is broad, so grant owners can get relief if they have a reason the city did not consider. 

“We’ll have to work through extensively over the next few months to put together criteria that we feel good about,” Bailey said. 

Ward 4 Councilor Randy Block felt uneasy about the plan’s intentionally vague criteria. 

“It seems to me that whatever hardship means that it needs to be grounded in the ordinance,” said Block. 

HCP applications are not expected for the next few years, explained Bailey.

The current successes and accomplishments of BERDO regulations were also discussed, including the topic of compliance training. 

This training is done to educate building owners, property managers, and facility professionals on how to comply with BERDO regulations, explained Bailey. These regulations also required disclosures on building utility information. 

Bailey reported that 49 out of 50 buildings disclosed required data.

“We feel pretty good about that, at least for a first year of getting the reporting word out there and teaching people how to do the reporting—it’s a good test run,” Bailey said.

Another key part of BERDO’s future is cementing its organizational efficiency. Bailey believes that his role is to get BERDO up and running so that it can better fulfill its mission. 

“[This goal requires] all of these little pieces of administrative work that need to get put into place,” Bailey said. 

While BERDO regulations are still in their infancy and will continue to evolve, reduced emissions and carbon neutrality remain the City Council’s chief aim.

The post Newton City Councilors Discuss New Emissions Regulations Under BERDO appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Newton Fair and Affordable Housing Approves Public Forum For Ballot Initiative
news_bc.author: Alison Palmieri
news_bc.summary:
The Newton Fair and Affordable Housing Partnership met Tuesday to vote on a plan for a public forum with the Newton League of Women Voters, hoping to better inform Newton residents on the upcoming Massachusetts rent control ballot initiative. 

The rent control proposal would cap rent increases in Massachusetts at 5 percent or the level of inflation—whichever is lower—and will be on the ballot during the upcoming November state elections.

Partnership member Grace Mitchell explained that she is concerned that the information about the ballot question has been incorrect and misleading at times, leading her to push for the public forum.

“I’m concerned personally that the information that I’m seeing about this measure is not all correct and is kind of misleading in a lot of cases,” Mitchell said. “And I feel like we can do something about that.”

The partnership aims to educate voters on the complex rent control ballot initiative by providing an unbiased presentation on the topic in the forum. The forum will feature experts from multiple sides of the issue and provide factual context through historical case studies to help residents make informed and thoughtful decisions, explained Mitchell. 

The partnership unanimously approved a motion to collaborate with the League of Women Voters on the forum. 

The event is intended to be a structured forum rather than a debate to keep the focus on objective sharing of information, explained partnership member Ann Houston.

“Part of the role of the partnership is to help make sure we’re making wise decisions, that people in Newton are in a position to make thoughtful decisions about housing issues,” Houston said.

The discussion then shifted to the Housing Priorities Task Force report, focusing on how the city can encourage developers to build larger, more affordable family units. Members suggested the city offer zoning bonuses or tax relief through Payments in Lieu of Taxes (PILOT). 

Member Steve West said that PILOTs are effective in helping the predictability of tax payments for affordable developments. 

“If you got a PILOT that you said you’re going to pay 10 percent—done,” West said. “Now I know what I’m going to pay in taxes. It really works.” 

Member Michael Lozano also noted that developers are often willing to provide higher affordability if the math makes sense for their projects. He mentioned that building larger units costs roughly $500 per square foot, which often outweighs the additional rent developers can collect.

“If you set the incentives correctly … that would get us what we want, a more diverse set of units, where a more diverse set of families could get affordable housing within Newton,” Lozano said. “It’s all math.”

To offset construction costs, Lozano suggested providing relief from Newton’s sewer connection and mitigation fees, which he claimed are “enormously higher” than in other communities. He argued that reducing fees would lower building costs to make cheaper housing possible without the city having to spend its own money.

The partnership then voted to send a letter to Newton Mayor Marc Laredo, highlighting the urgency of replacing recently retired ADA/Section 504 Coordinator Jini Fairley. Members argued that advocating for the position is essential for their work in maintaining a welcoming community, considering the partnership aims to ensure Newton remains a diverse and inclusive home for all residents. 

“If we’re interested in a city that is open and welcoming, we have to actually invest in that,” Houston said. “I think we don’t want to just check a legal box. This is really about the kind of community that I want to be living in and that I’d like to support.”

The post Newton Fair and Affordable Housing Approves Public Forum For Ballot Initiative appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: ‘Holy Days’ Fails To Commit to a Theme Despite Unique Storyline
news_bc.author: Ollie Lloyd
news_bc.summary:
★☆☆☆☆

Nat Boltt’s Holy Days, a film that tells the story of a young boy on a road trip with three elderly nuns, had the potential to be a unique and highly comical movie. Yet in its attempt to depart from convention, it ineffectively tried to marry too many different styles, tropes, and themes. 

The film follows a 10-year-old Māori boy named Brian (Elijah Tamati), who is both filled with grief over his mother’s death and bursting with anger at his father’s new fiancée. When Brian runs away from home, he miraculously finds refuge with a trio of elderly nuns. 

Sister Mary Clare (Jacki Weaver) presents as the kind, centering force of the trio, keeping them aligned with reality. Sister Agnes (Judy Davis) takes on a more rebellious role, smoking cigarettes and impulsively snapping into action. Sister Luke (Miriam Margolyes) struggles with dementia, often forgetting where she is and panicking throughout the story. 

After finding out their priest sold their convent to a developer, and that they would be sent to a nursing home, the group seeks out Patricia (Tanea Heke). A former nun—and Brian’s great aunt—Patricia holds evidence that could secure their rights to the property. Brian, still mourning his mother, tags along, bringing a youthful energy with the nuns and eliciting a nostalgic mood. 

Despite the unique plot, this film struggled to stick to a consistent theme. Holy Days often presents as a nostalgic family movie, then abruptly turns, becoming overly philosophical or sexual. The film occasionally uses cinematography styles similar to those employed by Wes Anderson, but then cuts to poorly animated shots. And, though it tries to address the pain that comes with dementia through Sister Luke’s character, the movie often turns her into a laughingstock instead. 

And, unfortunately, Tamati’s performance was similarly flawed. He often spoke or acted as a younger character than he is alleged to be, leading to confusing scenes and often cringy dialogue. 

The progression of Brian’s character was also disjointed, distracting viewers from the main plot. In the beginning, he presents as an immature, one-dimensional character. After a brief disappearance on a mountain where he passes out from the cold weather, he hallucinates “seeing” his mother. From this point on, he suddenly has an emotional depth—but, without any journey to accompany it. He forgives his stepmother and moves on from his mother’s death, all without much explanation. 

Brian also attempts to reconnect with his Māori identity by visiting a culturally significant mountain and reconnecting with his aunt. This theme is not really extrapolated, however, and instead feels forced inside the road trip plot. 

Despite the issues with the pacing and writing, Holy Days did often boast incredible visuals. Filmed in New Zealand, scenes showed jagged green mountains cutting into a turquoise sea.  which created beautiful contrast with the sister’s royal blue habits. 

Though some scenes were emotionally evocative, most failed in their attempt to capture authentic feeling. In one of the only successful sentimental moments, Sister Agnes, explaining the meaning of miracles to Brian, compares gifts from God to little doors. 

“If you don’t walk through them, nothing happens,” Sister Agnes says. Brian takes these words to heart, working to repair relationships in his life and amend his past wrongdoings. 

Still, a few heartfelt moments and pretty nature shots could not mask the film’s truly poor plot line. If the writers and directors had picked a lane and stuck with it, focusing on one theme, one cinematic style, and just one cliché trope, it would have done the film a lot of good. 

The post ‘Holy Days’ Fails To Commit to a Theme Despite Unique Storyline appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: No. 12 BC Rallies to Surge Past No. 15 Loyola Maryland With Big Second Quarter
news_bc.author: Henry Sheridan
news_bc.summary:
No. 12 Boston College women’s lacrosse turned an early deficit into a statement win on Saturday afternoon, riding a dominant second quarter and clutch late-game scoring to defeat No. 15 Loyola Maryland 11–8 and extend its winning streak to three.

The Eagles (8–5, 5–3 Atlantic Coast) entered the matchup with momentum after a season-best 22-goal outburst against Albany. And although the offense took time to ignite in Saturday’s ranked matchup, it ultimately delivered when it mattered most.

Loyola (9–5, 5–1 Patriot League) struck first with two quick goals in the opening five minutes from Morgan Ruhnke and Lauren Perfetto. After a brief lull, Mim Suares-Jury capitalized on a rebound opportunity to push the Greyhounds ahead 3–0 late in the first quarter.

BC finally broke through with just 45 seconds remaining in the period, as Kylee Colbert scored a much-needed first goal. That late spark carried into the second quarter, when the Eagles flipped the game entirely.

Molly Driscoll needed just 33 seconds to score. Just over a minute later, Marissa White found space in front of the net and buried a feed from Casey Colbert to tie the game 3–3. Shea Baker sparked the Eagles’ fourth goal by dispossessing Loyola, carrying the ball up the field, then finding Discroll, who fed Kylee Colbert for a score. Colbert’s goal gave BC its first lead of the afternoon.

“[Baker] just played like a senior, and I can see how coming on so strong,” BC head coach Acacia Walker-Weinstein said. “She’s carrying this team emotionally, and there’s no one else that I’d rather have doing it.”

White tacked on another goal to make it 5–3, and Hanna Davis caught fire late in the half, scoring twice in the final 4:09 to stretch the Eagles’ lead to four. Loyola halted the 7–0 run with a goal from Morgan Quade, but BC entered halftime firmly in control, up 7–4.

“We started getting in possession, and our team was making great stops,” Driscoll said. “We were just getting the ball. We were able to pull away after that.”

The second half opened with more of the same. Driscoll struck again with an overhead finish less than a minute in, setting the tone early. Loyola responded quickly with a goal from Ava Kane, but BC maintained its edge.

With less than three minutes remaining in the third quarter, Driscoll capitalized on a ground ball, quickly turning and firing it into the net to extend BC’s lead. Loyola answered with a free-position goal in the closing seconds of the period and added another early in the fourth to cut its deficit to 9–7.

But every time Loyola threatened to come back, BC had an answer. 

“I think it’s really just making sure that everyone’s running the offense, doing everything right, knowing their role and just like, really doing your job,” Driscoll said. “I think we were definitely humbled at the beginning [of the season] … we have really learned to take that adversity and put it into success.”

White completed her hat trick six minutes into the fourth quarter, restoring BC’s three-goal cushion. Suares-Jury notched her third of the game for Loyola shortly after, but White delivered the final blow with 1:17 remaining—a dagger that sealed the 11–8 victory.

The Eagles’ ability to control possession proved decisive. Despite facing the nation’s top draw control team, BC dominated the circle 13–8, which was critical in limiting Loyola’s opportunities. The Eagles also registered 21 ground balls to Loyola’s 12. 

“I was really proud of how we contributed defensively on the draw,” Walker-Weinstein said. “Lily [Kondas] and Abbey [Herod] did such a great job beating the number one team in the country.”

The win marks BC’s sixth in its last seven games. After weathering an early 0–3 record, the Eagles have proved they can adapt, respond, and close.

“Beginning of the season, we were losing,” said Driscoll. “But we really decided to turn a page, and I think it’s been up ever since.”

The post No. 12 BC Rallies to Surge Past No. 15 Loyola Maryland With Big Second Quarter appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: No. 22 BC Falls 8-7 to No. 6 UNC in Saturday Rubber Match
news_bc.author: Jake D'Angelo
news_bc.summary:
No. 22 Boston College baseball and No. 6 UNC split a pair of scrappy affairs on Thursday and Friday. The series-deciding match on Saturday was no different, with neither team giving an inch.

With a top-three spot in the ACC on the line, the Tar Heels (27–5–1, 11–4 Atlantic Coast) defeated the Eagles (22–11, 9–6) 8–7 in comeback fashion to take the weekend series. 

After registering just two runs on Friday, BC wasted no time getting on the board in a preview of the offensive explosion to come.

Back-to-back walks followed by back-to-back strikeouts to open the second inning brought veteran Kyle Wolff up to the plate for his first at-bat of the series. He roped a fastball into left field for a double, bringing Jack Toomey home for an early 1–0 lead. 

UNC pitcher Folger Boaz was able to get out of the inning without any more damage, forcing a Colin Larson groundout. Boaz would throw a strong 4.2 innings, giving up just three hits while striking out four.

After a quiet first two innings, BC Brady Miller ran into some trouble in the third. A Perry Hargett walk and a Jake Schaffner single put runners on the corners with one out for UNC. Gavin Gallaher swung at the first pitch he saw, sending a single into left field and evening the score at one. 

Two batters later, Macon Winslow lifted a two-RBI double into left–center. Owen Hull threatened to score another UNC run with a single, but Julio Solier who was able to throw Winslow out at home, bringing the third to an end with the home squad leading 3–1.

UNC threatened again in the fourth. A Colin Hynek double was followed by Tyler Howe being hit by pitch, putting two runners on with nobody out. But Brady Miller got out of the jam, earning a strikeout and inducing a groundball double play to end the fourth with no damage done.

BC’s offense picked back up in the fifth. A Gunnar Johnson walk and a Solier double put two Eagles in scoring position with Nick Wang coming up and Camron Seagraves replacing Boaz on the mound. 

Seagraves walked Wang in four pitches to load the bases, before Ty Mainolfi doubled into right to clear the bases and put BC back up 4–3. Two pitches later, Toomey brought Mainolfi home with a double to extend the lead to two. A Hendrickson walk ended Seagraves’ night after just 12 pitches, as Matthew Matthijs was brought out of the bullpen. 

The Eagles didn’t stop there. Luke Gallo came through with a single into left that drove Toomey home, before wild pitch brought Hendrickson home for BC’s sixth run of the inning. The inning finally ended after Gunnar Johnson grounded out with BC leading 7–3. 

Miller was pulled after putting two runners on base to open the sixth, ending his day with five innings pitched, two strikeouts, and eight hits surrendered. 

Kyle Kipp came in to replace Miller, and started his day by issuing a walk to bring the tying run to the plate with no outs. Tyler Howe reached on a fielder’s choice, bringing one runner home and leaving runners on first and third, and a sacrifice fly let the Tar Heels cut BC’s lead to two.

Kipp continued to struggle in the seventh, as he walked the bases full with one out before getting relieved by John Kwiatkowski. 

Kwiatkowski’s first pitch was a wild one, advancing each runner and cutting BC’s two-run lead in half. Three pitches later, Owen Hull chopped a single into right, bringing both Tar Heels home and putting UNC back up 8-7. The Tar Heels looked to extend their newfound lead, but a double play ended the inning.

Both teams were held scoreless in the eighth, giving the Eagles one last chance to fight for the series win in the ninth with the top of their order due up. 

Mainolfi recorded a two-out single, but Walker McDuffie struck Toomey out to end the game. McDuffie recorded his second save in as many days, preserving the Tar Heel lead and cementing the comeback as an 8–7 victory. 

The post No. 22 BC Falls 8-7 to No. 6 UNC in Saturday Rubber Match appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Boston College brings Brenden Straughn to Luke Murray’s men’s basketball staff
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Monday afternoon, it was reported by ESPN that incoming Boston College men’s basketball head coach Luke Murray is bringing on Brenden Straughn as an assistant coach.

Straughn comes from Syracuse’s staff, where he spent three seasons under head coach Adrian Autry before Autry was fired last month. Before coaching at Cuse, Straughn coached in high school and AAU from 2013-2018, then was on the coaching staff at Loyola Maryland, Saint Joseph’s, and George Washington. Reportedly, Straughn has deep recruiting ties to the DMV and DC area, and he could be an important leader for BC’s roster construction while Murray is busy in Indianapolis coaching UConn in the Final Four. The transfer portal opens tomorrow after the national championship game tonight.

Welcome, Coach Straughn!

news_bc.title: Boston College guard Donald Hand Jr. enters the transfer portal
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Monday, Boston College junior guard Donald Hand Jr. announced his decision to enter the transfer portal.

Hand was the ACC’s Most Improved Player in 2024-25 after a season in which he averaged 15.7 points per game to lead the Eagles. But his offense took a downturn this season as he went through plenty of cold streaks that sent his PPG and shot percentage down in a tailspin. He finished this season as BC’s second leading scorer with 12.4 points per game on 33% shooting, and his three-point percentage took a nosedive from 37% last season to 23% this season.

Good luck, DJ!

news_bc.title: Boston College guard Fred Payne enters the transfer portal
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Sunday, Boston College junior guard Fred Payne announced his decision to enter the transfer portal.

Payne was Boston College’s leading scorer in 2025-26, leading the Eagles offense with 15.8 points per game on 40% shooting from the field. He was one of the lone bright spots for a BC offense that finished 339th in the country in points scored. He will have two years of eligibility remaining and is electing to not be a part of Luke Murray’s program in Chestnut Hill.

Good luck, Fred!

news_bc.title: Boston College Men’s Basketball’s Boden Kapke Declares Intent to Transfer
news_bc.author: Curran Schestag
news_bc.summary:
On Saturday morning, news broke that Boston College F Boden Kapke will not be returning to the Heights next year.

Kapke came to BC last offseason after a pair of seasons at Butler. He started the year on the bench before working his way into the starting rotation, and he was BC’s third-leading scorer at 10.6 points per game, to go with 5.7 rebounds and 0.7 assists. Given his status as one of the more productive players on last year’s roster, his loss is probably BC’s biggest to date as Luke Murray begins his rebuilding of the program. Boden always represented BC well, and we wish him luck at his next stop!

news_bc.title: Report: TD Garden to host Boston College Men’s Basketball vs UMass Next Season
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Thursday, it was reported that Boston College and UMass are planning a match-up next season at TD Garden between their men’s basketball teams.

The time and date are not officially announced. This would be the first time Boston College has played at TD Garden in 12 years, following their match-up with UMass in November 2014. This report follows another recent report that Providence and Syracuse plan to play a game at TD Garden next season also.

BC recently hired new head coach Luke Murray, an assistant from UConn, who will be leading the Eagles in his first season when this game is expected to occur.

news_bc.title: BC’s Luka Toews enters the transfer portal
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Wednesday, it was reported that Boston College sophomore guard Luka Toews has entered the transfer potral.

Toews was one of BC’s few three-point threats this past season, shooting 39% from behind the arc on 2.7 attempts per game. He started 20 of his 29 games for Boston College and made an impact for a BC offense that often struggled to find open shots. The Japan native also improved to average 2.2 assists and 1.6 rebounds per game. Good luck at your next stop, Luka!

news_bc.title: Luke Murray Officially Introduced as Boston College Men’s Basketball Head Coach
news_bc.author: Curran Schestag
news_bc.summary:
On Tuesday afternoon, Luke Murray officially took the helm of the Boston College Men’s Basketball program, addressing the media at his introductory press conference.

With Boston College entering a new era, here are three of my takeaways from the conference:

This program is going to look different, on and off the court

Murray made that very clear. Off the court, Murray talked extensively about turning the perception of the program around. Winning cures all, of course, and Murray acknowledged that — “just give us a chance” he asked of the students and fans — but above all, the team will be “prepared.”

One of the reasons Murray was such an attractive candidate to BC is his reputation as an offensive savant. His presser made clear that the brand of basketball he is bringing to Chestnut Hill will be “aesthetically pleasing;” one that fans will want to watch and players will want to play in. More importantly, however, Murray articulated that “we are going to dive into an analytical model, in terms of the way we play and the way we roster construct. We’re going to have a very healthy shot diet; it’s going to be one that mathematically makes sense.” Expect a much different style of play than what BC fans have been used to recently.

Winning is the goal

    On the surface level, this is obvious — why would winning not be the goal? Yet perhaps the biggest storyline leading up to Murray’s hire was whether or not BC was going to pony up the resources Murray felt were necessary to win. Literally hours before the news about Murray’s hiring broke, Dan Hurley discussed how he felt his assistants were ready for jobs of their own — provided that the program was completely invested in competing and winning at the highest level.

    When asked today about whether he’s bee given assurances of enough financial and administrative support to win, Murray answered that he has — and that there “are plans in place to become more and more competitive.” And I’m inclined to believe him. Murray was making almost a million dollars at UConn, working with perhaps the best coach in the country. He had no shortage of interested programs in the past few years. The fact that he picked BC tells me that the university is at least making an attempt to be competitive.

    The work starts now

    Murray will be staying with UConn through their postseason run. He described the past few weeks as “wild;” he signed his BC contract in D.C. preparing for the Huskies’ Sweet 16 matchup.

    He’ll fly with UConn to Indianapolis tomorrow, and will be back in Boston no later than next Monday. He knows the enormity of the task ahead; the transfer portal officially opens on Monday. Most of his staff seems to be falling into place, but there’s a lot to be done to get this program off the ground.

news_bc.title: Boston College hires Kate Popovec-Goss as Head Women’s Basketball Coach
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Friday evening, it was announced that Kate Popovec-Goss has been hired to replace Joanna Bernabei-McNamee as the head coach of the Boston College women’s basketball program. The full press release from BC can be found here.

Popovec-Goss has been the head coach of the Bradley Braves of the Missouri Valley Conference for the past four seasons, staging a quick turnaround from a 4-28 record in her first season to a 20-13 record in 2025-26. Before her stint at Bradley, she was an assistant at Northwestern, La Salle, and Colgate.

According to a report by USA Today, Boston College told potential candidates that $500,000 is available for roster construction through revenue sharing and NIL, which is in the bottom third of the ACC.

Welcome to the Heights, Coach Popovec-Goss!

news_college_hockey.title: Alaska Anchorage coach Matt Shasby resigns
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Alaska Anchorage is searching for a head coach.

Matt Shasby has resigned from his position, the university announced Monday, bringing an end to a four-year tenure that began at a critical juncture in the program’s history.

“It’s the right time for me to pursue some other opportunities in my life, personally,” Shasby told the Anchorage Daily News. “I think where we were four years ago, when we had no players and no games scheduled, and kind of just bringing the team back from basically starting over to where it is now, I think we’re in a great spot.”

Shasby was hired to lead Alaska Anchorage following the program’s revival and tasked with rebuilding from the ground up. Over four seasons, he compiled a 34-86-9 record, with the Seawolves showing their most progress during the 2023–24 campaign, when they finished 15-17-2.

“We appreciate Matt’s service to the hockey team and the university,” said acting athletic director Tanya Pont. “He stepped in during an important chapter for the program, and we wish him the best in his future endeavors.”

Alaska Anchorage officials said a national search for the program’s next head coach will begin immediately.

We’ll have more on this story in our next newsletter.

news_college_hockey.title: Trump's executive order on college sports isn't likely to hold up, but it could be his signal for Congressional assistance
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
We’re one week out from the national championship game. But before the Frozen Four takes center stage, there’s a wave of news from across the college hockey landscape worth unpacking.

In today’s edition of College Hockey Insider (#669):

  • Reaction to the Trump administration’s executive order issued Friday, which aims to reestablish structure in college athletics, including proposed changes to eligibility, transfers, NIL, and more.

  • The transfer portal is now open for graduate students and players impacted by coaching changes (or program discontinuations). A link to my 2026 portal tracker, along with a full list of entries to date, is included.

  • St. Cloud State is closing in on its next head coach, with finalist interviews set for this weekend.

  • A look at NCAA regional television ratings.

  • Updates on staff shakeups, along with a key retention at Brown.

  • Ethen Wyttenbach never seriously considered entering the transfer portal. Meanwhile, Chris Pelosi is set to return for the Bobcats.

  • The U.S. Collegiate Selects will return to the Spengler Cup for the next two years.

  • A top NHL Draft prospect has committed to Denver.

Trump’s executive order on college sports is likely a roadmap for Congress

I’ve spent much of the last 24 hours poring over President Donald Trump’s executive order, issued late Friday, that aims to bring some structure back to NCAA regulations.

We’ll get to the highlights of the order in a moment. But first, it’s worth examining the order itself.

In the executive order, Trump directs the NCAA to revert to some of its previous rules — particularly regarding transfers — by August 1. However, many of those same rules have already been challenged in court and deemed unlawful.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Notebook: What's the latest at St. Cloud, can players who sign pro now return to school?
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
With some unexpected travel to Sioux Falls last week, I’m still catching up on some news and notes from around the country.

So for today’s newsletter, I’m going to empty my notebook and play catch-up all in one issue.

The Huskies are moving quickly to fill their head coaching vacancy after Brett Larson departed for the Minnesota job last week.

Nick Oliver could emerge as the top candidate. Oliver, 34, is a young coach and alum who is currently on Mike Hastings’ staff at Wisconsin. That could complicate things from an announcement standpoint with Wisconsin in the Frozen Four, but I don’t think it would be out of the question to make the announcement and clarify that he would start at St. Cloud following the conclusion of Wisconsin’s season.

He’s not Wisconsin’s head coach, so I don’t think it would be much of a distraction. For instance, UConn assistant Luke Murray just took the Boston College basketball job this week, and he’ll coach in the Final Four this weekend for the Huskies before reporting for duty with the Eagles.

Oliver was at St. Cloud as an assistant under Larson from 2018–22 before becoming a head coach in the USHL with the Fargo Force. After one year in Fargo, he took the Wisconsin position under Hastings, and he’s now in his third season with the Badgers.

Oliver isn’t the only candidate considered a finalist.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Frozen Four Field Set
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
The Frozen Four field is set.

Michigan held off a late push from Minnesota Duluth on Sunday night, while Denver knocked off Western Michigan to punch its ticket to Las Vegas in two weeks.

Frozen Four Semifinals

When: Thursday, April 9
Where: T-Mobile Arena (Las Vegas)

Wisconsin vs. North Dakota, 5 p.m. ET
Michigan vs. Denver, 8:30 p.m. ET

news_college_hockey.title: Friday at the Regionals: Denver and Western will meet again
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Denver and Western Michigan will meet again tomorrow in Loveland with a trip to the Frozen Four on the line. Meanwhile, in Albany, Michigan got past Bentley, and Minnesota Duluth ended Penn State’s season.

Here’s a recap of everything that happened on Friday night in the regionals.

Loveland Regional

Western Michigan 3, Minnesota State 1: Western Michigan seized control early and never relinquished it, building a two-goal lead in the opening period.

Zaccharya Wisdom and Zach Bookman struck in the opening minutes to give the Broncos immediate momentum.

Minnesota State pushed back in the third, as Alex Zetterberg cut the deficit to one with an early goal, but the Broncos absorbed the pressure and closed it out. Owen Michaels sealed the win with an empty-netter.

“The more you possess, the more you have it, that’s the best defense in hockey,” WMU coach Pat Ferschweiler said.

Minnesota State’s season still ends with hardware, as the Mavericks captured the CCHA championship last week. There were questions about the program’s trajectory following Mike Hastings’ departure to Wisconsin, but Luke Strand has kept the program squarely in the national picture.

“I’m grateful to be at Minnesota State, I’m grateful to serve these players and this program,” Strand said. “I’m a very relational coach; I’m going to miss the guys that are leaving. But what they’ve done and left behind for us to learn and grow from is elite. We have to learn internally, getting here is one thing and moving on is another thing.”

Denver 5, Cornell 0: Denver delivered a complete performance in its win over Cornell.

Johnny Hicks was sharp once again, turning aside all 24 shots he faced.

“I thought we were excellent from the first drop of the puck,” DU coach David Carle said. “Our speed was there with and without the puck. I thought the guys executed at a really high level.”

The Pioneers showcased their depth, getting goals from five different players.

“We hadn’t played a team that had 38 goals from the defense corps before,” Cornell coach Casey Jones said. “I’ll be honest, that is, like, three times as much as most teams. And it comes off No. 23 (Eric Pohlkamp) and it comes off No. 9 (Boston Buckberger). They get pucks down to the net. They’re hard to defend.”

Albany Regional

Michigan 5, Bentley 1: The Wolverines advanced to the regional final, but the margin on the scoreboard masked a far tighter contest. Michigan held a 29–25 edge in shots, while Bentley finished just minus-1 in expected goals.

The difference was execution.

Michigan capitalized on its opportunities and punished mistakes. T.J. Hughes scored his 21st goal of the season, Jack Ivankovic turned aside 24 shots, and Jayden Perron chipped in with two assists to pace the win.

“There were some situations in which, late in the period, that they took advantage of that were big plays, and that’s a lethal group of players,” Bentley coach Andy Jones said. “They take a small crack, and they drive right through it, and they were opportunistic tonight.”

Minnesota Duluth 3, Penn State 1: Penn State grabbed the early lead on a goal from Shea Van Olm, but Minnesota Duluth methodically took control as the game progressed.

Max Plante pulled the Bulldogs even late in the first period with his 25th goal of the season. The breakthrough came in the third, when Hunter Anderson converted off a turnover by Gavin McKenna, who attempted a backhand pass in transition that never connected. Anderson made no mistake, burying the chance with 5:13 remaining.

Ty Hanson added an empty-net goal to seal it.

In net, Adam Gajan delivered a composed 29-save performance for UMD, which now advances to face Michigan in Sunday’s regional final.

“You never know what to expect right in these games, and a lot of them end up turning out that way,” UMD coach Scott Sandelin said. “Just glad it turned out in our favor, but our guy was good, their guy was good, and that’s what you need to win games this time of year. You need really good goaltending.”

This is issue #667 of the College Hockey Insider newsletter.

Thanks for reading and being part of this community — your support truly means everything. Whether you’ve been here from the start or just joined us, we’re grateful to have you along for the ride.

If you have ideas for a future newsletter, tips to pass along, or questions for a mailbag, I’d love to hear from you. You can reach me anytime at mike@collegehockeyinsider.com. Sponsorship inquiries are also welcome.

You can also find me on X at @MikeMcMahonCHN.

news_college_hockey.title: Here's what I know on Mercyhurst dropping its hockey program
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Mercyhurst announced Friday morning that it is dropping men’s hockey as part of a broader restructuring at the university.

The news came as a shock to players — and to head coach Tom Peffall, who was set to take over next season for Rick Gotkin following his retirement.

In a statement, Mercyhurst said it “will support all affected student-athletes by honoring athletics scholarships, providing academic advising, and assisting students who choose to pursue opportunities through the NCAA transfer portal.”

Division I women’s hockey will remain unaffected.

What makes this situation more troubling is how the decision was handled.

Just two days earlier, the university released a video as part of its annual Giving Day, soliciting financial support for the hockey program, among other initiatives.

According to a player who requested anonymity, players were encouraged to reach out to family and friends for donations for Giving Day this week. They were then informed of the decision abruptly, without any opportunity for input.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Thursday Recap: All three Hockey East teams eliminated on Day 1
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
The first day of the NCAA Tournament is in the books, and it was a difficult one for Hockey East, which saw all three of its teams eliminated. Meanwhile, Worcester will send a Big Ten representative to the Frozen Four on Saturday, while North Dakota will face Quinnipiac in Sioux Falls.

Worcester Regional

Michigan State 2, UConn 1: The Huskies went 0-for-5 on the power play, which ultimately proved to be the difference. UConn outshot Michigan State, 42-22, but couldn’t solve Spartans goaltender Trey Augustine, who made 41 saves. Ryker Lee and Porter Martone scored for Michigan State.

“I thought UConn was the better team tonight,” Michigan State coach Adam Nightingale said. “Trey was the best player on the ice by a country mile for us and I have a lot of respect for how (UConn) played. We got ourselves into a lot of penalty trouble, and we have to clean that up a bit.”

Wisconsin 5, Dartmouth 1: Wisconsin broke the game open in the third period with four unanswered goals — including two empty-netters — to pull away from the Big Green. Dartmouth was limited to just 14 shots on goal. Simon Tassy scored twice for Wisconsin, while Quinn Finley recorded three points (1g, 2a).

“We took some time off,” Wisconsin coach Mike Hastings said. “The group was able to spend some time together and this group has always had each other’s back, so for us to get off to a good start was very important for us. (Dartmouth) is incredibly disciplined, so for us to take advantage of that early power play, it gave us a big momentum boost.”

Sioux Falls Regional

Quinnipiac 5, Providence 2: The Friars made it interesting late, cutting a 3-0 deficit to 3-2 in the third period before the Bobcats added two empty-net goals. Quinnipiac scored three times in the second period after a scoreless first.

“It wasn’t perfect. We had some bad penalties late, we had a bad turnover late. So we need to grow up a little bit, mature if we want to get back to the Frozen Four,” Bobcats coach Rand Pecknold said. “When you’re playing at this level with this much pressure, and there’s a lot of things going through these young kids’ minds, you got to handle adversity. And we did a good job of that.”

North Dakota 3, Merrimack 0: Jan Špunar posted his fifth shutout of the season, stopping all 31 shots he faced — his highest save total in a shutout this season.

The Warriors pushed late, outshooting North Dakota 14-4 in the third period, but Špunar held firm.

“When we play a strong game in front of him, he rarely lets bad goals in. He just makes all the saves that he should,” North Dakota coach Dane Jackson said. “I thought it was an outstanding response by him where there were maybe a couple people saying, ‘[He] hasn’t played so well,’ but it was more on our team kind of being a little more committed and more detailed.”

news_national.title: Michigan muscles its way to program's 2nd NCAA basketball title, beating UConn
news_national.author: The Associated Press
news_national.summary:
High-scoring Michigan had to get down and dirty to dig out the national title Monday, making only two 3-pointers all night but still muscling its way to a 69-63 victory over stingy, stubborn UConn.

(Image credit: Michael Conroy)

news_national.title: What can Artemis II astronauts see that satellites haven't captured?
news_national.author: Ava Berger
news_national.summary:
The astronauts on Artemis II will observe parts of the moon rarely seen by human eyes. A NASA planetary scientist said it will offer a vital perspective for lunar research.

(Image credit: NASA)

news_national.author: Camila Domonoske
news_national.summary: With gasoline prices averaging above $4 a gallon nationally, drivers are grappling with a sharp rise in fuel costs. How can you get the most out of every fill-up?

news_national.title: Even when Arsenio Hall's show was a hit, 'everyone wanted it to be something else'
news_national.author: Tonya Mosley
news_national.summary:
Hall's late-night show gave hip-hop a home on TV and helped propel Bill Clinton to the White House. "I wanted to do this show that didn't exist when I was a kid," he says. Hall's memoir is Arsenio.

(Image credit: Kevin Winter)

(Image credit: Charles Krupa)

news_ktn.summary:
Matthew Farwell’s DNA was on strap used to asphyxiate Sandra Birchmore, prosecutors say

BREAKING NEWS ALERT
The DNA of former Stoughton police Officer Matthew Farwell was found on the strap used to asphyxiate Sandra Birchmore, the young pregnant woman he was allegedly having an affair with, according to new evidence disclosed by federal prosecutors in court filings Monday.

news_ktn.author: msprvs1=20556ejtv5JIt=bounces-1-3@bounce.nytimes.com (msprvs1=20556ejtv5JIt=bounces-1-3@bounce.nytimes.com)
news_ktn.summary:
Breaking news: Artemis II calls home after emerging from moon’s far side
The astronauts concluded a historic lunar flyby.
View in browser

April 6, 2026, 7:37 p.m. ET

BREAKING NEWS

NASA, via Associated Press

NASA Astronauts Call Home After Emerging From Moon’s Far Side

The astronauts were cut off from the rest of humanity as their spaceship slipped behind the moon, severing radio communications with mission control. During their planned interlude in silence, the astronauts reached their greatest distance from Earth, 252,756 miles.

Follow live updates

news_ktn.title: Breaking News: Trump renews threat to destroy Iranian bridges and power plants if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened by Tuesday night
news_ktn.author: 0101019d649d59a4-aef4941d-1683-4dac-b424-d84b8c21e1b1-000000@us-west-2.washingtonpost.com (0101019d649d59a4-aef4941d-1683-4dac-b424-d84b8c21e1b1-000000@us-west-2.washingtonpost.com)
news_ktn.summary:
